response:
**Code review – `POST /api/reset-password`** | # | Issue | Why it’s a problem | How to fix it (concrete steps) | |---|-------|--------------------|--------------------------------| | 1 | **No input validation / sanitisation** | `email` is taken straight from `req.body`. A malformed or missing value will cause `db.findUser(undefined)` (or throw) and may leak internal errors. | • Validate that `email` exists and matches a reasonable RFC‑5322 regex (or use a library like `validator.isEmail`).
• Trim whitespace.
• Return `400 Bad Request` with a clear message if validation fails. | | 2 | **User‑enumeration via timing / response** | The endpoint always returns `{ success: true }` even if the user does **not** exist. An attacker can infer whether an email is registered by measuring response time or by checking if the email‑send function throws. | • Always perform the same amount of work: look up the user (or a dummy record) **and** call `sendResetEmail` regardless of whether a real user was found.
• Return a generic response (`{ message: "If the account exists, you will receive an email." }`) with status 200 for both cases. | | 3 | **Insecure token generation** | `Math.random()` is **not** cryptographically secure; it is predictable and unsuitable for security‑critical tokens. Using `.toString(36).substr(2)` also reduces entropy. | • Use Node’s crypto module: `const token = crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex');` (or `base64url`).
• Store a **hashed** version of the token in the database (see #5). | | 4 | **Token never stored / verified** | The generated token is only used to send an email; there is no record of it server‑side, so the reset link cannot be validated later. | • After generating the token, compute a hash (`crypto.createHash('sha256').update(token).digest('hex')`) and store that hash together with an expiry timestamp and a user‑ID reference.
• When the user clicks the reset link, compare the supplied token’s hash with the stored hash. | | 5 | **No token expiration / replay protection** | A token that lives forever (or until manually cleared) can be reused indefinitely, enabling account takeover if the email is intercepted. | • Store an `expiresAt` timestamp (e.g., `now + 1 hour`).
• On verification, reject tokens where `Date.now() > expiresAt`.
• After a successful password reset, delete or invalidate the token record.
